August: 19, 1977
Terry c. Andrus
Deputy County Counsel
Orange county
P.
or
Box 1379
Santa Ana, CA
92702
Re:
Opinion Request Noi 77»020
Dear Mr.
Andrus:
Thank you for your letter of July 27,
1977i
YDu re-
quest an opinion concerning the reporting obligations that
arise from the establishment of
a legal defense fund on be-
half of Ralph Diedrich,
Orange County Supervisnr.
Mr,
Diedrlch
was
lndicted on July
1,
1977,
for alleged violations of
the
reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act.
You ask
whether munies donated
tn help defer Mr,
Diedrlch's
legal EX-
penses are reportable as "income" under chapter 7 of the Act
or as "campaign contributions" under chapter
4.
Because your
question does not raise a substantial question under the Act,
no opinion will be issuadi
however,
I hope you find the fol-
lowing comments helpfuli
The commission has adopted a regulation,
2 call Adm.
Code Section 18215, which defines a campaign contribution to
include:
.
l
i any monetary or non—monetary payment for
which full and adequate Consideration is not
made to the donut
that is received Dr made at
the behest of:
(1)
A candidate,
unless it 15 clear
frcm surrounding circumstances that the
payment was received or made at his hehest
for personal purposes unrelated to his
Candidacy or status as an officeholderl
,
.
.
2 Cal.
Adm.
Code
Sl3215{b’


Terry c. Andrus
,
F
page M
August
l9,
1977
Since the
indictment of Mr.
Diedrich relates to his
obligations as an Officeholder,
the standard set forth
in the
regulation is met so long as Mr. Diedrich is considered a
"candidate."
Government Code Section 32007 defines
"candidate"
to mean:
i
.
i
an
individual
i
.
.
who receives a contri-
butian or makes an expenditure or gives his
con~
sent for any Ether oerscn to receive a contribu-
tion or make an expenditure with a View to bring-
ing about his nomination or election to any elec-
tive office,
whether Or not the specific elective
office for which he will seek nomination or elec—
tion
is known at me time the contribution
is re-
ceived or the expenditure is made and whether or
not he has announced his candidacy or filed
a
declaration of candidacy at such
time.
You indicate
in your letter than
"Koln different
occasions, the supervisor has publicly expressed an intent not
to run for reelection,
as well as an intent
(:6 run under cer-
tain conditions.
His present term expires in 1950‘"
Since the
possibility exists
that Mr.
Diedricn Will run again for elec~
tive office,
and since,
although he is apparently unsure,
he
has not categorically ruled out that possibility, we would re—
gard him as
a
"Candidate" within
the meaning of Section 52007i
The
fact that the outcome of his
trial could
influence voter
opinion on this question strengthens our view that money col-
lected for this purpose will be more political than personal
in nature.
This
interpretatian Of SectiDn 82007
is Consistent
with previous opinions of
the Commission which have held that
leftaover campaign funds which are used by an Officeholder to
support political activities,
such as traveling to speeches
and meetings and printing newsletters, retain their political
Character and should be reported as Chapter
4
transactions
even though the election Ear which the
funds were raised has
been concluded.
See opinions requested by Janet Gray Hayes.
1 FPPC Opsl
210
(NO.
75-145,
Dec.
4,
1975)
and Willie L.
Brown. Jri,
l 1:ch Ops. 67
(No. 75—055, July 2,
1975’.
The raising of money for
a legal defense fund be—
cause of possible violations of the Political Reform Act by
a current officehalder seems similarly political in nature.
Accordingly, the appropriate place to disclose these transac—
tions would be on a campaign statement filed pursuant to
chapter 4.


Terry c. Andrus:
i»,
Page three
August lg,
1977
If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal, Adm. Code Section
18321.
If you have any questions,
please Contact Michael
J.
Baker,
Chief of the Commission's Legal
Divisiani

Re:
Request for Opinion
Hawa‘wustasw
Gentlemen:
File New
P-297
On behalf of Philip L. Anthony, orange county supervisor, and
pursuant to California Government Code Section 83114 and
2 California
Administrative code Section 18320. your opinion and advice is re-
quested concerning Supervisor Anthony's duties under the PolitiCal
Reform Act of 1974 under the following
facts:
supervisor Anthony and others were indicted by the Orange
county Grand Jury on July 1, 1977, for alleged violations
of
the Political Reform Act.
A number of individuals have
expressed interest in creating a legal defense fund, wherein
the monies raised would be used for the supervisor's legal
defense,
and surplus funds,
if any,
would be either returned
to the donors on a pro tats basis or used by the Supervisor
to help finance his future campaigns,
It
is contemplated
that contributions
for such fund will be received by an
existing committee which has filed a Form 410,
"Committee
statement of organization," with the secretary of state.
The question presented for your opinion and advice is whether
monies received for the above-stated purpose are to be considered
"income"
reportable under Chapter
7 of the Act,
or
”campaign contri-
butions"
reportable under Chapter
4.
Your prompt attention to this request will be greatly apprev
ciated.
Very truly yours,
ADRIAN KUYPER,
COUNTY COUNSEL
By (SEW 2am
WWW/sac
Laurence M. Watson, Deputy
7
"
cc:
Supervisor Philip L. Anthony

sections.conclusion: The appropriate place to disclose these transactions would be on a campaign statement filed pursuant to Chapter 4. The Commission regards the supervisors as "candidates" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82007, and the raising of money for a legal defense fund because of possible violations of the Political Reform Act by a current officeholder is political in nature, not personal.
sections.facts:
supervisor Anthony and others were indicted by the Orange
county Grand Jury on July 1, 1977, for alleged violations
of
the Political Reform Act.
A number of individuals have
expressed interest in creating a legal defense fund, wherein
the monies raised would be used for the supervisor's legal
defense,
and surplus funds,
if any,
would be either returned
to the donors on a pro tats basis or used by the Supervisor
to help finance his future campaigns,
It
is contemplated
that contributions
for such fund will be received by an
existing committee which has filed a Form 410,
"Committee

The question presented for your opinion and advice is whether

reportable under Chapter

or

reportable under Chapter

Your prompt attention to this request will be greatly apprev
ciated.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether legal defense fund contributions for indicted county supervisors should be reported as personal income under Chapter 7 or as campaign contributions under Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that legal defense fund contributions should be reported as campaign contributions under Chapter 4 because the supervisors remain candidates and the funds are political in nature given the connection to violations of the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document contains multiple letters from August 1977. Primary FPPC response is the August 19 opinion letter addressing Diedrich case (Opinion Request 77-020). Secondary follow-up letter of August 25 addresses similar Anthony case but references the Diedrich opinion. OCR quality varies; some text distortion present but main sections are legible. No formal CONCLUSION section heading in either letter, but advisory opinions clearly stated.

CONCLUSION: The appropriate place to disclose these transactions would be on a campaign statement filed pursuant to Chapter 4. The Commission regards the supervisors as "candidates" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82007, and the raising of money for a legal defense fund because of possible violations of the Political Reform Act by a current officeholder is political in nature, not personal.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides advice on two related requests regarding reporting obligations for legal defense funds established on behalf of Orange County Supervisors Ralph Diedrich and Philip L. Anthony, who were indicted for alleged violations of the Political Reform Act. The Commission concludes that contributions to these defense funds should be reported as campaign contributions under Chapter 4, not as personal income.
